enrichment.short_description: AI-powered website visitor identification and intent data platform that de-anonymizes B2B site traffic and automatically triggers sales outreach.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan for up to 500 de-anonymized visitors per month; paid plans start in the hundreds of dollars per month
enrichment.seo_title: Warmly Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Warmly does, how its visitor identification and intent data pricing works, and whether this AI sales orchestration platform fits your GTM stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Warmly is a B2B go-to-market platform, founded around 2019 in San Francisco by Maximus Greenwald and Carina Pontillo, that identifies anonymous website visitors and turns that traffic into pipeline. It combines website visitor de-anonymization (identifying both companies and, where possible, individual contacts visiting a site), intent data aggregation from providers such as Clearbit, Demandbase, Apollo, and Bombora, and AI-driven orchestration that automatically triggers outbound actions while a visitor is still on the site. Typical workflows include alerting an assigned sales rep in Slack the moment a high-intent account lands on a pricing page, launching an AI chatbot conversation, queuing a personalized email, or adding the visitor to a LinkedIn outreach sequence. Warmly went through the Y Combinator Summer 2020 batch and has raised venture funding including a Series A round backed by investors such as RTP Global, Felicis, and NFX, with total funding in the tens of millions of dollars. The company is generally described as a mid-size startup with roughly 100 employees as of recent reporting. Warmly targets B2B SaaS revenue teams, particularly sales development, demand generation, and revenue operations functions at small and mid-market companies that want to convert anonymous website traffic into identified pipeline without manually building outbound lists. The platform integrates with common CRM and sales engagement tools and with Slack for real-time alerting. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free tier reveals a limited number of de-anonymized visitors per month, while paid plans scale visitor volume, add multi-channel orchestration (chat, email, LinkedIn), and unlock more CRM and enrichment integrations. Reported paid tiers have ranged from several hundred dollars per month for growth-stage teams up to enterprise pricing for larger visitor volumes and more advanced AI agent features. Because Warmly relies on IP-to-company matching and third-party enrichment data, identification accuracy and coverage vary by traffic source, company size, and geography, and person-level identification is inherently more limited than company-level identification due to privacy constraints. Warmly is best understood as part of the broader website visitor identification and intent data category, competing with tools like Clearbit Reveal, RB2B, Leadfeeder, and 6sense on the specific angle of pairing identification with automated, in-the-moment orchestration rather than just reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Warmly
paragraphs: Warmly is a website visitor identification and sales orchestration platform built for B2B revenue teams. It watches traffic on a company's website, matches anonymous visitors to companies and, where data allows, individual contacts, and layers on intent signals so sales and marketing teams can see who is actively researching their product.

What sets Warmly apart from a plain visitor-identification tool is the orchestration layer on top of the data. Instead of only surfacing a report of who visited, Warmly can immediately act on that information: notifying a rep in Slack, starting a live AI chat with the visitor, queuing an email sequence, or adding the contact to a LinkedIn outreach campaign, all while the visitor is still browsing the site.

Founded around 2019 and based in San Francisco, Warmly went through Y Combinator and has raised institutional funding to build out its AI agent and orchestration capabilities, positioning itself against both traditional visitor-identification vendors and newer AI sales development tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Warmly's core capability is company and person-level visitor identification, pulling from multiple third-party data providers to enrich anonymous traffic with firmographic and contact details.

On top of identification, the platform adds real-time intent scoring so teams can prioritize accounts showing buying signals such as repeat visits, pricing-page views, or engagement with specific content.

Orchestration features let teams configure automated plays that trigger the moment a qualifying visitor is detected, including Slack alerts to the right rep, AI chatbot engagement on-site, automated email sequences, and LinkedIn connection or messaging workflows.

Warmly also integrates with common CRMs and sales engagement platforms so identified visitors and intent signals flow directly into existing pipeline and reporting processes rather than living in a separate dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Warmly uses a freemium pricing model. The free plan allows a limited number of de-anonymized visitors per month, which is enough for smaller sites or teams evaluating the product before committing to a paid tier.

Paid plans scale primarily on monthly visitor volume and the depth of orchestration features included, such as the number of automated plays, AI chat usage, and CRM or Slack integrations. Reported starting prices for paid tiers have been in the range of a few hundred dollars per month, with higher tiers and enterprise pricing available for companies with larger traffic volumes or more advanced automation needs.
As with most usage-scaled B2B tools, exact current pricing is best confirmed directly on Warmly's pricing page, since visitor thresholds and feature bundling have changed as the product has evolved.

features.description: Surfaces buying-intent signals such as pricing-page visits and repeat sessions to help teams prioritize outreach.

features.title: AI chat orchestration
features.description: Engages qualifying visitors with an on-site AI chatbot in real time while they are still browsing.

features.title: Slack alerting
features.description: Notifies the assigned sales rep instantly in Slack when a target account or high-intent visitor is detected.

features.title: Automated outbound sequencing
features.description: Queues email and LinkedIn outreach automatically based on visitor behavior, reducing manual list-building.

features.title: CRM and data provider integrations
features.description: Connects to common CRMs and enrichment sources like Clearbit, Apollo, and Bombora to keep pipeline data current.

pros: Combines identification and orchestration in one platform rather than requiring separate tools
Free tier makes it easy to test visitor identification value before paying
Real-time Slack alerts help reps act on hot accounts immediately
Multiple identification data sources can improve match coverage
AI chat and sequencing reduce manual outbound work for smaller sales teams
cons: Person-level identification coverage is inherently limited by data availability and privacy constraints
Pricing scales with visitor volume, which can get expensive for high-traffic sites
Match accuracy depends on third-party data providers and can vary by industry or region
Best value requires an existing CRM and sales workflow to plug the orchestration into

faqs.question: What does Warmly actually do?
faqs.answer: Warmly identifies anonymous companies and, where possible, individuals visiting a website, adds intent signals, and automatically triggers sales and marketing actions like Slack alerts, AI chat, email, and LinkedIn outreach.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Warmly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Warmly offers a free plan that reveals a limited number of de-anonymized visitors per month, suitable for testing the product on smaller traffic volumes.

faqs.question: How accurate is Warmly's visitor identification?
faqs.answer: Accuracy varies by traffic source and company size. Company-level identification is generally more reliable than person-level identification, which depends on third-party contact data availability.

faqs.question: Does Warmly integrate with my CRM?
faqs.answer: Warmly integrates with common CRM and sales engagement tools so identified visitors and intent data can flow into existing pipeline workflows.

faqs.question: Who is Warmly built for?
faqs.answer: Warmly targets B2B sales, marketing, and revenue operations teams, particularly at SaaS companies, that want to convert anonymous website traffic into identified, actionable pipeline.

faqs.question: How is Warmly different from other visitor identification tools?
faqs.answer: Warmly emphasizes real-time orchestration on top of identification, automatically triggering outreach actions while the visitor is still on the site, rather than only providing an identification report.

faqs.question: Does Warmly replace a sales development rep?
faqs.answer: Warmly automates some outbound tasks like alerting and initial outreach, but it is generally used to augment a sales team rather than fully replace human sales development roles.

enrichment.short_description: WhatsApp Business API platform for customer engagement, marketing broadcasts, and support with AI chatbots and a shared team inbox.
enrichment.starting_price: Growth plan from around $59 per month (annual billing), plus WhatsApp per-message fees
enrichment.seo_title: WATI Review 2026: WhatsApp Business API Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore WATI's WhatsApp Business API platform, including pricing tiers, chatbot and shared inbox features, and how message fees are billed.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WATI (an acronym for WhatsApp Team Inbox) is a WhatsApp Business API platform built to help businesses manage customer communication, marketing broadcasts, and support at scale over WhatsApp. It was founded around 2020 and is operated out of Hong Kong by Clare.AI Limited, a company that originally built conversational AI for the financial services sector before pivoting to focus on WhatsApp Business API tooling for small and mid-size businesses. WATI provides a shared team inbox where multiple support and sales agents can manage WhatsApp conversations from one interface, no-code chatbot and flow builders for automating FAQs and lead qualification, broadcast campaign tools for marketing and notification messages, integrations with e-commerce and CRM platforms such as Shopify and HubSpot, and AI-powered automation features for response suggestions and customer segmentation. The company reports serving well over ten thousand businesses across more than 100 countries, spanning industries like e-commerce, education, healthcare, and real estate that rely on WhatsApp as a primary customer communication channel, particularly in regions such as Southeast Asia, India, Latin America, and the Middle East where WhatsApp adoption is very high. WATI's pricing follows a subscription model with multiple tiers, commonly named Growth, Pro, and Business, priced roughly between $50 and $300 per month on annual billing, scaling by the number of agent seats, number of WhatsApp number channels, and access to advanced features like chatbots, forms, and round-robin conversation routing. Critically, the subscription fee is separate from WhatsApp's own conversation-based messaging charges set by Meta, which vary by recipient country and message category (marketing, utility, or authentication); WATI and most WhatsApp Business Solution Providers add a markup, commonly cited around 20 percent, on top of Meta's base per-conversation rates. There is generally no permanent free tier, though a short free trial is typically available. WATI competes in the WhatsApp Business API and conversational commerce space against providers like Twilio, Interakt, AiSensy, Zoko, and Gupshup.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WATI
paragraphs: WATI is a WhatsApp Business API platform that gives businesses a shared inbox, automation tools, and broadcast capabilities for managing customer conversations over WhatsApp at scale. The name stands for WhatsApp Team Inbox, reflecting its origin as a tool for teams to collaboratively manage WhatsApp support and sales chats.

The platform is built and operated by Clare.AI Limited, a Hong Kong-based company, and has grown to serve a large base of businesses worldwide, with particularly strong adoption in markets where WhatsApp is the dominant messaging channel for commerce and customer service.

Beyond basic messaging, WATI layers on no-code chatbot building, AI-assisted responses, contact segmentation, and integrations with e-commerce and CRM tools, positioning it as a full customer engagement platform built specifically around the WhatsApp Business API rather than a generic omnichannel inbox.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: A shared team inbox lets multiple agents manage WhatsApp conversations from a single dashboard, with assignment, tagging, and internal notes to coordinate handoffs.
No-code chatbot and flow builders allow businesses to automate FAQs, lead capture, appointment booking, and order status updates without writing code.

Broadcast campaign tools support sending WhatsApp template messages for marketing, promotions, and transactional notifications to segmented contact lists, subject to WhatsApp's opt-in and template approval rules.
Integrations with platforms like Shopify, WooCommerce, HubSpot, and Zapier connect WhatsApp conversations to existing e-commerce and CRM data.
AI features assist agents with suggested replies and automate parts of the conversation flow, reducing manual response time for common queries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WATI charges a monthly or annual subscription fee for platform access, typically structured across three tiers, commonly named Growth, Pro, and Business, that differ by the number of included agent seats, WhatsApp number channels, and available automation features.

On annual billing, published pricing has generally ranged from roughly $50 to $60 per month for the entry Growth tier up to several hundred dollars per month for the Business tier, with additional agent seats available at extra cost on each plan.

Separate from the platform subscription, WhatsApp itself charges per-conversation fees that vary by the recipient's country and the message category (marketing, utility, or authentication). WATI, like other WhatsApp Business Solution Providers, passes these fees through with a markup, commonly cited at around 20 percent above Meta's base rates, so total cost depends heavily on messaging volume and geography.

No-code chatbot builder makes automation accessible to non-technical teams
Strong fit for regions and industries where WhatsApp is the primary customer channel
Wide range of e-commerce and CRM integrations
Flexible pay-as-you-go structure with no long-term cancellation penalties
cons: No permanent free plan, only a short free trial
Total cost is harder to predict because WhatsApp per-message fees are billed on top of the subscription
Additional agent seats add extra cost on top of the base plan
Advanced automation and multi-number routing are locked behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: What is WATI used for?
faqs.answer: WATI is used to manage customer support, sales conversations, and marketing broadcasts over the WhatsApp Business API from a shared team inbox with chatbot automation.

faqs.question: Does WATI charge for WhatsApp messages separately?
faqs.answer: Yes. WATI's subscription covers platform access, while WhatsApp conversation fees set by Meta are billed on top, typically with a markup of around 20 percent over Meta's base rates.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for WATI?
faqs.answer: WATI offers a 7-day free trial on its paid plans, but it does not offer a permanent free tier.

faqs.question: What are WATI's main pricing tiers?
faqs.answer: WATI is commonly sold in Growth, Pro, and Business tiers, differing by seat count, WhatsApp number channels, and access to advanced automation and routing features.

faqs.question: Can WATI build chatbots without code?
faqs.answer: Yes, WATI includes a no-code chatbot and flow builder for automating FAQs, lead capture, and common customer workflows.

faqs.question: Does WATI integrate with Shopify or HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Yes, WATI offers integrations with e-commerce platforms like Shopify and WooCommerce, as well as CRM tools like HubSpot, along with Zapier for broader connections.

faqs.question: Who operates WATI?
faqs.answer: WATI is operated by Clare.AI Limited, a company based in Hong Kong.

enrichment.short_description: Free accounting and invoicing software for small businesses, offering bookkeeping, payments, and payroll, owned by H&R Block.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan); Pro plan from $19 per month
enrichment.seo_title: Wave Accounting Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Wave's free accounting and invoicing software works, what Pro and Payroll cost, and whether it fits your small business bookkeeping needs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wave, marketed at waveapps.com, is accounting, invoicing, and financial software built for small businesses, freelancers, and sole proprietors. Wave Accounting Inc. was founded in Toronto, Canada, in 2009 by Kirk Simpson and James Lochrie, and launched publicly in 2010 with a free bookkeeping and invoicing product funded initially through a freemium model that later monetized via payment processing and payroll add-ons. In June 2019, tax preparation company H&R Block announced it would acquire Wave for approximately 405 million US dollars (roughly 537 million Canadian dollars), and the acquisition closed in July 2019; Wave has operated as a subsidiary of H&R Block since then while continuing to run out of Toronto. At the time of acquisition, Wave reported serving more than 400,000 small businesses across over 200 countries. The core Wave platform covers double-entry accounting, unlimited invoicing and estimates, receipt scanning, income and expense tracking, and financial reporting, with an add-on Wave Payroll product for running payroll and handling payroll tax calculations and filings in supported US states and Canadian provinces, plus Wave Payments for accepting online card and bank payments on invoices. Wave's pricing structure centers on a free Starter plan that covers manual bookkeeping, invoicing, and expense tracking without automatic bank feeds or receipt scanning, and a paid Pro plan priced around 19 US dollars per month (about 25 Canadian dollars) that adds automatic bank transaction imports, receipt scanning, multiple users, and removal of Wave branding from invoices. Wave Payroll is billed separately as an add-on with a base monthly fee plus a per-employee or per-contractor charge, and Wave also offers optional bookkeeping support services staffed by human bookkeepers for businesses that want hands-on help, priced at a higher monthly rate. Wave targets solo entrepreneurs, freelancers, and very small businesses that need straightforward accounting without the complexity or cost of platforms like QuickBooks or Xero, and it remains one of the best-known free options in the small business accounting software category.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wave
paragraphs: Wave is accounting and invoicing software designed for freelancers, sole proprietors, and small businesses that need to manage bookkeeping without the cost or complexity of larger enterprise accounting platforms. Founded in Toronto in 2009 and launched publicly in 2010, Wave built its early reputation on offering core accounting and invoicing tools for free, monetizing instead through optional payment processing and payroll services.

In 2019, tax preparation giant H&R Block acquired Wave for roughly 405 million US dollars, and Wave has continued to operate as part of H&R Block since then, retaining its own brand, product, and Toronto base while gaining access to H&R Block's resources and tax expertise.

Today Wave serves hundreds of thousands of small businesses with a product suite spanning accounting, invoicing, receipt scanning, online payments, and payroll, aimed squarely at very small businesses and solo operators rather than mid-market or enterprise companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Wave's core accounting engine supports double-entry bookkeeping, a chart of accounts, and financial reports like profit and loss, balance sheet, and cash flow statements, suitable for tracking a small business's full financial picture.
Unlimited invoicing and estimates let users create and send professional invoices, set up recurring billing, and accept online payments directly from an invoice when Wave Payments is enabled.
Receipt scanning, available as a paid add-on on the free plan and included in Pro, lets users photograph or upload receipts and have expense data extracted automatically.
Wave Payroll, sold as a separate add-on, handles employee and contractor payments, automatic payroll tax calculations, and tax filings in supported regions.
Bank connections on the Pro plan automatically import and categorize transactions from linked bank and credit card accounts, reducing manual data entry compared to the free Starter plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wave's Starter plan is free and covers manual bookkeeping, unlimited invoicing, and expense tracking, though it requires manual bank statement imports rather than automatic bank feeds and does not include receipt scanning without a paid add-on.

The Pro plan costs around 19 US dollars per month (about 25 Canadian dollars) and adds automatic bank transaction imports, built-in receipt scanning, multiple user roles, and removes Wave branding from invoices and other customer-facing documents.

Wave Payroll is priced separately from the accounting plans, with a base monthly fee plus a per-employee or per-contractor charge, and Wave also offers optional professional bookkeeping services with human bookkeepers at a higher monthly price point for businesses that want ongoing hands-on support.

Fewer advanced features and integrations compared to QuickBooks or Xero
Payroll is only available in a limited set of US states and Canadian provinces
Customer support on the free plan is more limited than on paid tiers

faqs.question: Is Wave accounting software really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes double-entry bookkeeping, unlimited invoicing, and expense tracking, though it lacks automatic bank feeds and built-in receipt scanning.

faqs.question: How much does Wave Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Wave Pro costs around 19 US dollars per month (about 25 Canadian dollars), adding automatic bank imports, receipt scanning, and removal of Wave branding.

faqs.question: Does Wave offer payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wave Payroll is a separate add-on with a base monthly fee plus a per-employee or per-contractor charge, available in supported US states and Canadian provinces.

faqs.question: Who owns Wave?
faqs.answer: Wave has been owned by H&R Block since 2019, when H&R Block acquired the company for approximately 405 million US dollars.

faqs.question: Who is Wave best suited for?
faqs.answer: Wave is best suited for freelancers, sole proprietors, and very small businesses that need straightforward accounting and invoicing without enterprise-level complexity.

faqs.question: Can I accept online payments through Wave?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wave Payments lets customers pay invoices online by card or bank transfer directly from the invoice.

faqs.question: Where is Wave headquartered?
faqs.answer: Wave is headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, Canada.

enrichment.short_description: Open source unified communications platform for building programmable voice, video, messaging, and call center infrastructure.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); commercial hosting and support available through Wazo.io
enrichment.seo_title: Wazo Platform Review 2026: Open Source PBX and UC Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Wazo Platform offers as an open source unified communications and PBX system, its architecture, pricing, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wazo Platform is an open source unified communications (UC) and PBX system for building carrier-grade, programmable IP telephony infrastructure. It was created in November 2016 in Quebec City, Canada, by a development team led by Sylvain Boily, who had earlier founded the XiVO open source telephony project in 2005; Wazo effectively continues and modernizes that lineage after the shutdown of its predecessor company, Proformatique Inc. Wazo is built primarily on top of the Asterisk PBX engine along with components like Kamailio and RTPEngine, and it exposes REST APIs, WebRTC, WebSockets, and webhooks so developers can integrate and extend telephony, chat, video conferencing, call center queues, voicemail, and presence features into other applications rather than treating the PBX as a closed appliance. The platform can be deployed on bare metal servers, virtual machines, or containers, and its distinctive components are distributed under the GPLv3 open source license, keeping the core stack free to self-host and modify. In addition to the open source community project at wazo-platform.org, there is a related commercial entity, Wazo (via wazo.io), that offers a white-label unified communications platform aimed at managed service providers (MSPs) and carriers who want to resell hosted PBX and UC services under their own brand, layering commercial support, hosting, and multi-tenant management on top of the open source foundation. Wazo's feature set targets class 5 telephony capabilities typically associated with carrier-grade phone systems: audio and video calling, team chat, conferencing, call queues and call centers, voicemail, and mobility features for remote and softphone users. Because it is open source and self-hostable, Wazo appeals most to telecom-savvy organizations, MSPs, developers building custom communications products, and organizations wanting to avoid vendor lock-in associated with proprietary hosted PBX or UCaaS providers. There is no fixed subscription price for the open source core; costs instead come from self-hosting infrastructure, optional commercial support contracts, or white-label hosted plans through the Wazo.io commercial offering, which are typically quoted per deployment or per seat for MSP and carrier customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wazo
paragraphs: Wazo Platform is an open source unified communications and PBX system for building programmable IP telephony infrastructure. Rather than functioning as a closed, proprietary phone system, Wazo is designed API-first, giving developers REST APIs, WebRTC, and webhooks to integrate calling, messaging, and video into their own applications and workflows.

The project traces its roots back to XiVO, an open source telephony system started in France in 2005, which later merged with Avencall before its team relaunched as Wazo in November 2016 out of Quebec City, Canada, following the shutdown of Proformatique Inc.

Wazo is built on the Asterisk PBX engine combined with Kamailio and RTPEngine, and its core distinctive components are released under the GPLv3 license, making the platform free to self-host, inspect, and modify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Wazo delivers class 5 telephony features including audio and video calling, voicemail, presence, and mobility for remote or softphone users, comparable to what a carrier-grade phone system provides.
Team chat and conferencing tools support internal collaboration alongside traditional voice calling within the same platform.
Call center functionality includes queues and routing logic suitable for support and sales teams handling inbound call volume.
An API-first architecture exposes REST APIs, WebRTC, WebSockets, and webhooks so developers can build custom integrations, embed calling into other software, or automate communications workflows.
Flexible deployment supports bare metal servers, virtual machines, or containers, giving organizations control over where and how the platform runs.
A related commercial offering, Wazo.io, provides a white-label version of the platform aimed at managed service providers and carriers who want to resell hosted UC services under their own brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Wazo Platform is free and open source under the GPLv3 license, so there is no license fee to self-host and run the system; costs come from your own infrastructure and any internal engineering time needed to deploy and maintain it.

Organizations that want commercial support, managed hosting, or a white-label multi-tenant offering can use the Wazo.io commercial product, which is aimed at MSPs and carriers and is typically priced per deployment or per seat through direct sales rather than published self-serve pricing.

Because Wazo is infrastructure software rather than a typical SaaS product, total cost of ownership depends heavily on deployment scale, hosting choices, and whether an organization opts for community support or a paid support contract.

features.title: Call center queues

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs on bare metal, virtual machines, or containers, giving organizations control over hosting.

features.title: Voicemail and presence
features.description: Includes standard PBX features like voicemail and user presence status across the organization.

features.title: White-label option via Wazo.io
features.description: Offers a commercial, white-label version aimed at MSPs and carriers reselling hosted UC services.

pros: Free and open source under GPLv3, with no per-seat licensing cost for the core platform
API-first design makes it well suited for developers embedding communications into custom applications
Built on the mature, widely deployed Asterisk PBX engine
Flexible deployment options including bare metal, VMs, and containers
White-label commercial option available for MSPs and carriers wanting a supported, brandable product
cons: Self-hosting requires telecom and Linux systems expertise, unlike plug-and-play hosted UCaaS products
No published self-serve pricing for the commercial white-label offering
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to larger open source or commercial UC platforms
Documentation and support quality can vary between the open source community edition and paid support

faqs.question: Is Wazo free to use?
faqs.answer: The core Wazo Platform is free and open source under the GPLv3 license. Commercial hosting, support, and white-label offerings through Wazo.io are separately priced.

faqs.question: What is Wazo built on?
faqs.answer: Wazo is built primarily on the Asterisk PBX engine, combined with components like Kamailio and RTPEngine for call routing and media handling.

faqs.question: Who uses Wazo?
faqs.answer: Wazo is used by developers building custom communications products, telecom-savvy organizations wanting to avoid vendor lock-in, and managed service providers reselling hosted UC services.

faqs.question: Where did Wazo come from?
faqs.answer: Wazo originated from the XiVO open source telephony project started in France in 2005 and was relaunched as Wazo in November 2016 by a team in Quebec City, Canada.

faqs.question: Can Wazo be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazo can be deployed on bare metal servers, virtual machines, or containers, and organizations are free to self-host the open source platform.

faqs.question: Does Wazo support video calling?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazo supports audio and video calling, team chat, and conferencing alongside its core telephony features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the open source project and Wazo.io?
faqs.answer: The open source project at wazo-platform.org is the free, self-hostable core, while Wazo.io is a commercial, white-label version aimed at MSPs and carriers who want hosted, brandable UC services.

enrichment.short_description: Open source security platform unifying SIEM and XDR for threat detection, log analysis, compliance, and incident response.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); Wazuh Cloud managed service priced on request
enrichment.seo_title: Wazuh Review 2026: Open Source SIEM and XDR Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Wazuh's open source SIEM and XDR platform works, what its free and cloud pricing looks like, and where it fits in a security stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wazuh is a free and open source security platform that unifies SIEM (security information and event management) and XDR (extended detection and response) capabilities for monitoring endpoints and cloud workloads. The Wazuh company was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in San Jose, California, growing out of an earlier fork of the OSSEC host-based intrusion detection project into a broader, actively maintained security monitoring platform. The Wazuh architecture consists of lightweight agents installed on monitored endpoints (servers, workstations, cloud instances, and containers) that collect logs, file integrity data, system inventory, and configuration information, which are then sent to a central Wazuh server for analysis, correlation, and alerting, with results visualized through an integrated dashboard built on OpenSearch. Core capabilities include log data analysis, file integrity monitoring, vulnerability detection, configuration assessment against benchmarks like CIS, intrusion and malware detection, incident response automation (active response), and regulatory compliance support for frameworks such as PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR. Because Wazuh is fully open source with no licensing cost, organizations can self-host the entire stack (agents, server, and dashboard) for free, an approach favored by security teams that want full data control, no vendor lock-in, or a lower-cost alternative to commercial SIEM platforms like Splunk or IBM QRadar. For teams that prefer not to manage the infrastructure themselves, Wazuh also offers Wazuh Cloud, a managed hosting and support service with custom, quote-based pricing rather than fixed public rates. The project has an active open source community, extensive documentation, and integrations with major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), container platforms, and threat intelligence feeds. Wazuh is widely used by security operations center (SOC) teams, managed security service providers (MSSPs), and IT teams at organizations of all sizes that need centralized threat detection and compliance monitoring without the licensing costs typically associated with enterprise SIEM and XDR products.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wazuh
paragraphs: Wazuh is a free, open source security platform that combines SIEM and XDR functionality to help organizations detect threats, monitor compliance, and respond to security incidents across endpoints and cloud workloads. The company behind the project was founded in 2015 and is based in San Jose, California.

The platform grew out of the open source intrusion detection ecosystem, evolving from an earlier OSSEC-based project into a broader, independently maintained security monitoring stack with its own agent, server, and dashboard components.

Because the entire stack is open source with no licensing fee, Wazuh has become one of the most widely adopted free security platforms, used by security teams that want full control over their data or a lower-cost alternative to commercial SIEM tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lightweight agents deployed on servers, workstations, cloud instances, and containers collect logs, system inventory, and file integrity data for centralized analysis.
Log data analysis and correlation identify suspicious activity and security events across the monitored environment, feeding into a central alerting and dashboard system.
File integrity monitoring tracks changes to critical files and configurations, helping detect unauthorized modifications or tampering.
Vulnerability detection scans installed software against known vulnerability databases to flag outdated or exploitable packages.
Configuration assessment checks systems against security benchmarks like CIS to identify misconfigurations.
Compliance support maps monitoring and reporting capabilities to regulatory frameworks such as PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR.
Active response features can automatically take action, such as blocking an IP address, when certain threats are detected.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wazuh's core platform, including the agents, server, and dashboard, is free and open source with no licensing cost, so organizations can deploy the full SIEM and XDR stack on their own infrastructure at no software charge.
Costs for a self-hosted deployment come from infrastructure (servers, storage, and scaling for log volume) and internal engineering time to deploy, tune, and maintain the platform.

For organizations that prefer a managed option, Wazuh Cloud offers hosted deployment and support, priced through custom quotes based on data volume, number of agents, and support level rather than fixed published pricing.

features.title: SIEM log analysis
features.description: Aggregates and correlates log data across endpoints and cloud workloads for centralized threat detection.

features.title: XDR endpoint protection
features.description: Extends detection and response capabilities across endpoints, cloud, and containers from a unified platform.

features.title: File integrity monitoring
features.description: Detects unauthorized changes to critical files and system configurations.

features.title: Vulnerability detection
features.description: Scans installed software for known vulnerabilities to help prioritize patching.

features.title: Configuration assessment
features.description: Checks systems against security benchmarks like CIS to identify misconfigurations.

features.title: Regulatory compliance reporting
features.description: Supports compliance monitoring and reporting for frameworks such as PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR.

features.title: Active response automation
features.description: Automatically executes response actions, such as blocking a malicious IP, when threats are detected.

pros: Fully open source with no licensing cost, unlike most commercial SIEM and XDR platforms
Broad feature set spanning log analysis, file integrity, vulnerability, and compliance monitoring
Active open source community with frequent updates and extensive documentation
Integrates with major cloud providers and container platforms
No vendor lock-in since organizations fully control their own deployment and data
cons: Self-hosted deployments require security and infrastructure expertise to set up and maintain
Managed Wazuh Cloud pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales quote
Scaling log ingestion for large environments can require significant infrastructure investment
Lacks some of the polished enterprise support and SLAs offered by large commercial SIEM vendors

faqs.question: Is Wazuh completely free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Wazuh platform is free and open source with no licensing cost. Costs come from your own hosting infrastructure, or from the optional paid Wazuh Cloud managed service.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Wazuh's SIEM and XDR capabilities?
faqs.answer: Wazuh's SIEM functionality focuses on aggregating and correlating log data for threat detection and compliance, while its XDR functionality extends detection and automated response across endpoints and cloud workloads in a unified platform.

faqs.question: Does Wazuh support compliance frameworks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazuh includes monitoring and reporting features that map to compliance frameworks such as PCI DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR.

faqs.question: Can Wazuh be deployed in the cloud?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazuh agents and integrations support major cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, and Wazuh also offers a managed Wazuh Cloud hosting option.

faqs.question: Who typically uses Wazuh?
faqs.answer: Wazuh is used by security operations center teams, managed security service providers, and IT teams that need centralized threat detection and compliance monitoring without commercial SIEM licensing costs.

faqs.question: How is Wazuh installed?
faqs.answer: Wazuh uses lightweight agents installed on endpoints that send data to a central Wazuh server, with results visualized through a dashboard built on OpenSearch.

faqs.question: How much does Wazuh Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Wazuh Cloud, the managed hosting option, uses custom pricing based on data volume and support needs rather than fixed published rates, so pricing requires contacting Wazuh directly.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source online whiteboard for real-time collaborative drawing and sketching, built and maintained by an independent developer.
enrichment.company_size: Independent open source project (single primary maintainer)
enrichment.seo_title: WBO Review 2026: Free Open Source Collaborative Whiteboard
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what WBO (Whiteboard Online) offers as a free, open source real-time collaborative whiteboard, plus how to use or self-host it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WBO, short for Whiteboard Online and also known by its open source project name whitebophir, is a free, simple, real-time collaborative whiteboard built and maintained by independent developer Ophir Lojkine. The publicly hosted demonstration instance runs at wbo.ophir.dev, while the underlying open source code is published on GitHub under the repository name whitebophir and is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPLv3). Unlike venture-backed collaboration platforms, WBO is a small, community-maintained tool rather than a company with employees, funding rounds, or a formal headquarters; it is best understood as a personal open source project that has grown a meaningful user base due to its simplicity and zero-cost access. The core product lets any number of users draw simultaneously on a shared virtual whiteboard, with changes synced in real time to everyone connected and the board's state persisted so work is not lost between sessions. To collaborate, a user simply shares the board's URL; boards can be created with a random, hard-to-guess name to function as a de facto private space accessible only to people who have the link, since there is no formal account or login system on the hosted version. The interface supports basic drawing tools, shapes, text, and image insertion, and the project has been translated into more than 15 languages, with reports that it ranks among the more popular free online whiteboard tools in some regions, including Russia and Ukraine. Because the code is open source and lightweight (built on Node.js), organizations or individuals who want a private, self-hosted whiteboard instance, for example for internal company use or to avoid relying on the public demo server, can deploy their own copy relatively easily, including via Docker. WBO does not offer paid plans, premium tiers, enterprise features, or SSO, and it lacks the polish, template libraries, and integrations of commercial whiteboard products like Miro or FigJam. It is best suited for lightweight, ad hoc collaborative sketching, teaching, brainstorming, or quick diagramming where cost, simplicity, and self-hosting matter more than advanced features.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WBO
paragraphs: WBO, short for Whiteboard Online, is a free, open source collaborative whiteboard that lets multiple people draw on the same virtual canvas in real time. It is built and maintained by independent developer Ophir Lojkine, with the code published under the project name whitebophir on GitHub.

The publicly hosted version runs at wbo.ophir.dev and requires no account or sign-up: users simply create a board and share its URL with collaborators to start drawing together, with the board's state automatically saved so work persists between sessions.

Because it is a lightweight, single-developer open source project rather than a funded company, WBO does not have the enterprise features, integrations, or support structure of commercial whiteboard tools, but it offers a genuinely free and self-hostable alternative for simple collaborative sketching.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Real-time collaborative drawing lets any number of connected users sketch on the same board simultaneously, with updates synced live to everyone viewing it.
Persistent board state means drawings are automatically saved, so a board's content survives after users close their browser and can be revisited later using the same URL.
Link-based access control lets users create boards with random, hard-to-guess names, functioning as a lightweight private space without requiring formal accounts or logins.
Basic drawing tools cover freehand drawing, shapes, text, and image insertion, enough for sketching, diagramming, and teaching use cases.
Self-hosting support lets technical users or organizations deploy their own private instance of WBO on their own server, including via Docker, for full control over data and availability.
Multi-language support with translations in more than 15 languages makes the tool accessible to a broad international audience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WBO is entirely free to use on the public hosted instance at wbo.ophir.dev, with no paid plans, subscriptions, or premium feature tiers.
Because the underlying whitebophir code is open source under the AGPLv3 license, anyone can also self-host their own private instance for free, only incurring their own server hosting costs.
There is no enterprise or commercial pricing tier, reflecting WBO's nature as an independent open source project rather than a commercial software company.

features.description: Automatically saves board state so drawings remain accessible after closing and reopening the link.

features.description: Lets users create boards with random names that function as a private space accessible only via the shared URL.

features.title: Self-hosting support
features.description: Open source code can be deployed on a private server or via Docker for full control over data.

features.title: No sign-up required
features.description: Users can start drawing and collaborating immediately without creating an account.

pros: Completely free to use with no paid tiers or subscriptions
No account or sign-up required to start collaborating
Open source and self-hostable for organizations that want data control
Simple, lightweight interface that loads quickly and is easy to learn
Translated into more than 15 languages for broad accessibility
cons: Lacks advanced features, templates, and integrations found in commercial whiteboard tools
No formal access controls or authentication on the public hosted version beyond the board link
Maintained largely by a single independent developer, which may affect long-term support pace
Not designed for enterprise use cases like SSO, audit logs, or team administration

faqs.question: Is WBO really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, WBO is completely free to use on the public hosted instance, and the underlying code is open source so anyone can self-host it for free as well.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use WBO?
faqs.answer: No, WBO does not require an account or sign-up. You simply create a board and share its URL with collaborators.

faqs.question: Who maintains WBO?
faqs.answer: WBO is built and maintained by independent developer Ophir Lojkine under the open source project name whitebophir.

faqs.question: Can I self-host WBO?
faqs.answer: Yes, the whitebophir code is open source under the AGPLv3 license and can be deployed on your own server, including via Docker.

faqs.question: How private are WBO boards?
faqs.answer: Boards can be created with a random, hard-to-guess name so they function as a private space accessible only to people who have the link, though there is no formal login-based access control.

faqs.question: What can WBO be used for?
faqs.answer: WBO is commonly used for teaching, brainstorming, quick diagramming, and general collaborative sketching where a lightweight, free tool is sufficient.

faqs.question: How does WBO compare to tools like Miro?
faqs.answer: WBO is much simpler and entirely free, but it lacks the templates, integrations, and enterprise features found in commercial whiteboard platforms like Miro or FigJam.

enrichment.short_description: All-in-one patient communication, phone, and payments platform built for dental, veterinary, and other healthcare practices.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (quote required); commonly reported in the range of $100 to $500 per month depending on practice size
enrichment.seo_title: Weave Review 2026: Patient Communication Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Weave's phone, texting, scheduling, and payments platform works for dental and healthcare practices, plus pricing and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Weave, at getweave.com, is an all-in-one patient communication and practice growth platform built primarily for dental and veterinary practices, along with other healthcare and small business verticals. The company, Weave Communications, was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Lehi, Utah, and has grown into a publicly traded company (NYSE: WEAV) with roughly a thousand or more employees serving practices across North America. Weave's platform combines cloud-based VoIP phone service with two-way texting, missed-call auto-text, call recording, and team chat, layered on top of deep integrations with practice management software so staff can see patient information the moment a call comes in. Beyond communication, Weave includes online scheduling, automated appointment reminders, insurance verification workflows, review generation and reputation management, and payment tools such as card terminals, manual card entry, card-on-file billing, buy-now-pay-later style payment plans, and text-to-pay links that let patients settle bills directly from a text message. The platform is positioned as a way for small and mid-size healthcare practices, especially dental and veterinary offices, to reduce front-desk administrative burden, fill more open appointments, and improve the patient experience without stitching together separate phone, scheduling, and payment vendors. Weave has grown partly through acquisitions, including companies focused on AI-powered scheduling and communication automation such as TrueLark, expanding its automation capabilities for handling routine patient interactions like booking and rescheduling without staff involvement. Pricing is not published publicly; Weave sells through a quote-based, custom pricing model tailored to practice size, specialty, and the specific bundle of communication, scheduling, and payment features selected, with third-party estimates commonly placing typical monthly costs somewhere between roughly 100 and 500 dollars, though actual quotes vary by practice. Weave competes in the healthcare-focused patient communication and practice management software category against products like RevenueWell, Solutionreach, and Podium, differentiating primarily through its combined phone system plus payments plus communication bundle purpose-built for practice management software integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Weave
paragraphs: Weave is an all-in-one communication, scheduling, and payments platform built specifically for dental, veterinary, and other healthcare practices. Founded in 2008 and headquartered in Lehi, Utah, the company has grown into a publicly traded business serving thousands of practices across North America.

At its core, Weave replaces a practice's traditional phone system with a cloud-based VoIP solution that integrates directly with practice management software, so staff see patient records and appointment history the moment a call or text comes in.

Beyond phones and texting, Weave bundles in online scheduling, automated reminders, review management, and a suite of payment tools, aiming to consolidate the many separate vendors a small healthcare practice might otherwise use into one platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cloud-based VoIP phone service includes call recording, missed-call auto-text, and team chat, replacing a practice's legacy phone system with one built for healthcare front desks.
Practice management integrations pull up patient records automatically when a call or text comes in, saving front-desk staff from manually looking up patient information.
Online scheduling and automated appointment reminders reduce no-shows and let patients book appointments without calling the office directly.
Insurance verification workflows help front-desk staff confirm patient coverage before appointments, reducing billing surprises.
Payment tools include card terminals, manual card entry, card-on-file billing, buy-now-pay-later style payment plans, and text-to-pay links for settling bills via text message.
Review generation and reputation management features help practices collect and respond to patient reviews across platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Weave does not publish fixed pricing; the platform is sold through a custom, quote-based model tailored to a practice's size, specialty, and the specific mix of phone, scheduling, and payment features selected.

Third-party estimates commonly place typical monthly costs somewhere in the range of 100 to 500 dollars per practice, though actual pricing depends heavily on the number of locations, phone lines, and add-on modules chosen.

Prospective customers generally need to request a demo and quote directly from Weave's sales team rather than self-serve signing up, which is typical for practice management and healthcare communication software in this category.

Backed by a large, publicly traded company with ongoing product investment

Primarily built for healthcare verticals, so it may be less suited to unrelated industries

Feature bundles and pricing can vary significantly by practice size and specialty

faqs.question: What is Weave used for?
faqs.answer: Weave is used by dental, veterinary, and other healthcare practices to manage phone calls, texting, scheduling, reminders, and payments in one platform integrated with practice management software.

faqs.question: How much does Weave cost?
faqs.answer: Weave uses custom, quote-based pricing rather than published rates, with third-party estimates commonly in the range of 100 to 500 dollars per month depending on practice size and features.

faqs.question: Does Weave replace my office phone system?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weave provides a cloud-based VoIP phone system intended to replace a practice's traditional phone line, adding features like call recording and missed-call auto-text.

faqs.question: Can patients pay their bill through Weave?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weave includes payment tools such as text-to-pay, card terminals, manual card entry, and card-on-file billing.

faqs.question: Does Weave integrate with practice management software?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weave integrates with most dental and veterinary practice management software to display patient information automatically during calls and texts.

faqs.question: Is Weave a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weave Communications is publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker WEAV.

faqs.question: Where is Weave headquartered?
faqs.answer: Weave is headquartered in Lehi, Utah, United States.

enrichment.short_description: Open source AI-native vector database for building semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, and generative AI applications.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source, self-hosted); Weaviate Cloud from around $25 per month
enrichment.seo_title: Weaviate Review 2026: Open Source Vector Database Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Weaviate's open source vector database works for AI search and RAG, plus its self-hosted and cloud pricing options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Weaviate is an open source, AI-native vector database designed for building semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and other generative AI applications at scale. The company was founded in 2019 by Bob van Luijt (CEO) and Etienne Dilocker (CTO) and is headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands, operating as a remote-first team with employees across many countries. Weaviate stores both data objects and their vector embeddings together, combining vector similarity search with structured filtering, keyword-based (BM25) search, and hybrid search in a single query interface, along with built-in support for reranking and generative search modules that connect retrieved results directly to large language models for RAG pipelines. The core database is released under the Apache 2.0 open source license and can be self-hosted on a developer's own infrastructure, integrated with popular machine learning model providers (such as OpenAI, Cohere, and Hugging Face) for generating embeddings, and used through client libraries in languages including Python, JavaScript, Go, and Java. The company has raised significant venture funding, including a 16 million dollar Series A round in February 2022 led by NEA and a 50 million dollar Series B round in April 2023 led by Index Ventures with participation from Battery Ventures and existing investors, funding used to expand engineering and to build out Weaviate Cloud, the company's managed hosting offering. Weaviate's pricing spans three tiers: the open source database is free to self-host with only infrastructure costs; Weaviate Cloud offers a free sandbox for experimentation plus paid Standard tier plans generally in the range of 25 to 200 dollars per month priced by vector dimensions stored (commonly cited around 0.095 dollars per million vector dimensions), with Professional and Business Critical tiers starting around 135 and 450 dollars per month respectively for production workloads requiring higher availability and support; and custom enterprise pricing for large-scale or dedicated deployments. Weaviate competes in the fast-growing vector database category against products like Pinecone, Milvus, Qdrant, and Chroma, and is commonly used by developers and AI teams building semantic search engines, recommendation systems, chatbots, and RAG-based applications on top of large language models.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Weaviate
paragraphs: Weaviate is an open source, AI-native vector database built for semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, and other applications powered by large language models. Founded in 2019 by Bob van Luijt and Etienne Dilocker and headquartered in Amsterdam, the company has grown into one of the better-known independent vector database providers as demand for AI infrastructure has expanded.

The core Weaviate database stores both structured data objects and their vector embeddings together, allowing developers to combine vector similarity search with traditional filtering and keyword search in a single query rather than stitching together separate systems.

Weaviate is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it free to self-host, while the company also operates Weaviate Cloud, a managed hosting service, and has raised significant venture capital, including a 50 million dollar Series B round in 2023, to fund product development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hybrid search combines vector similarity search with keyword-based BM25 search in a single query, improving relevance for use cases where pure semantic search alone falls short.
Built-in generative search modules connect retrieved results directly to large language models, streamlining retrieval-augmented generation pipelines without extra integration work.
Model integrations support popular embedding providers such as OpenAI, Cohere, and Hugging Face, letting developers generate and store vectors without managing separate embedding infrastructure.
Structured filtering allows queries to combine vector similarity with traditional metadata filters, such as category or date range, for more precise results.
Reranking support improves result ordering after initial retrieval, which is useful for RAG applications where result quality directly affects LLM output.
Multi-language client libraries in Python, JavaScript, Go, and Java make it accessible across common application development stacks.
Cloud-native architecture supports horizontal scaling and fault tolerance for production workloads handling large volumes of vector data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Weaviate database is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, so developers can self-host it on their own infrastructure at no software cost, paying only for their own compute and storage.

Weaviate Cloud, the managed hosting option, includes a free sandbox tier for experimentation, followed by paid Standard tier plans generally priced in the range of 25 to 200 dollars per month, billed roughly by vector dimensions stored.

Higher Professional and Business Critical tiers, starting around 135 and 450 dollars per month respectively, add production-grade availability, support, and scaling for larger workloads, while large enterprise deployments are priced through custom quotes.

features.title: Hybrid vector and keyword search
features.description: Combines semantic vector search with BM25 keyword search in a single query for improved relevance.

features.title: Built-in RAG support
features.description: Connects retrieved results directly to large language models for retrieval-augmented generation pipelines.

features.title: Embedding model integrations
features.description: Integrates with providers like OpenAI, Cohere, and Hugging Face to generate vector embeddings automatically.

features.title: Structured filtering
features.description: Combines vector similarity search with traditional metadata filters for more precise query results.

features.title: Reranking
features.description: Improves the ordering of retrieved results after initial vector search, boosting relevance for downstream applications.

features.title: Multi-language client libraries
features.description: Provides SDKs in Python, JavaScript, Go, and Java for integration across common development stacks.

features.title: Horizontal scaling
features.description: Supports cloud-native, fault-tolerant scaling for production workloads with large vector datasets.

pros: Open source core is free to self-host under the Apache 2.0 license
Hybrid search combining vector and keyword search improves relevance over pure vector search alone
Built-in integrations with major embedding model providers reduce setup complexity
Strong fit for retrieval-augmented generation applications built on large language models
Managed Weaviate Cloud option removes the need to operate infrastructure for teams that prefer it
cons: Self-hosting at scale requires infrastructure and operational expertise
Cloud pricing based on vector dimensions can be harder to estimate upfront than flat per-seat pricing
Competes in a crowded vector database market with strong alternatives like Pinecone and Qdrant
Some advanced features and higher-tier support require paid Weaviate Cloud plans

faqs.question: Is Weaviate free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Weaviate database is open source and free to self-host under the Apache 2.0 license. Weaviate Cloud also offers a free sandbox tier alongside paid plans.

faqs.question: What is Weaviate used for?
faqs.answer: Weaviate is used to build semantic search, recommendation systems, chatbots, and retrieval-augmented generation applications powered by large language models.

faqs.question: Does Weaviate support hybrid search?
faqs.answer: Yes, Weaviate combines vector similarity search with keyword-based BM25 search in a single hybrid query.

faqs.question: How much does Weaviate Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Weaviate Cloud pricing generally ranges from around 25 to 200 dollars per month on the Standard tier, with Professional and Business Critical tiers starting around 135 and 450 dollars per month respectively, plus custom enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Weaviate?
faqs.answer: Weaviate was founded in 2019 by Bob van Luijt (CEO) and Etienne Dilocker (CTO) and is headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

faqs.question: What embedding models does Weaviate support?
faqs.answer: Weaviate integrates with popular embedding model providers such as OpenAI, Cohere, and Hugging Face, along with support for custom or self-hosted embedding models.

faqs.question: Can Weaviate be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, since the core database is open source under Apache 2.0, it can be self-hosted on a developer's own infrastructure as an alternative to using Weaviate Cloud.

use_cases.title: Semantic search applications
use_cases.description: Developers build search experiences that understand meaning and context rather than relying solely on exact keyword matches.
use_cases.audience: Application developers and search engineers

use_cases.title: Retrieval-augmented generation for LLMs
use_cases.description: AI teams use Weaviate to retrieve relevant context that is fed into large language models to improve accuracy and reduce hallucination.
use_cases.audience: AI and machine learning engineering teams

use_cases.title: Recommendation and personalization systems
use_cases.description: Companies use vector similarity search to power product or content recommendations based on user behavior and preferences.
use_cases.audience: Product and data engineering teams
